county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Talbot County formed 1662 from Kent County. County seat is Easton, the 'Colonial Capital of the Eastern Shore.' Wealthy tobacco and grain county with prominent planter families. Frederick Douglass was born near Easton (c. 1818). Named for Grace Talbot, daughter of George Calvert, 1st Baron Baltimore.
county_metadata.research_tips: MSA and Talbot County Circuit Court (Easton). Formed 1662 from Kent County on the Eastern Shore. Land records on mdlandrec.net from the 1660s. Talbot County is central to the history of slavery and emancipation in Maryland — Frederick Douglass was born near Easton (c. 1818) and his autobiographies provide rich genealogical context. The Talbot County Free Library has a Maryland Room with local resources. The Historical Society of Talbot County in Easton holds family files, photographs, and local records. Quaker meeting records (Third Haven Meeting, 1682) document early Quaker families and their enslaved persons' manumission records. Strong tobacco and grain economy with wealthy planter families well-documented in estate records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Talbot land records on mdlandrec.net; MSA has digitized deed books, mortgages, and land patents; one of the most complete online land record systems in the US

source_profiles.source_name: Talbot Land Records -- Maryland State Archives / mdlandrec.net

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records for Talbot on mdlandrec.net; digitized deed books, mortgages, and land patents; MSA has indexed and imaged virtually all Maryland land records online through mdlandrec.net, one of the best state land record systems in the US

source_profiles.repository: Maryland State Archives, Annapolis / Talbot Clerk of Circuit Court

source_profiles.source_name: Talbot County Church Records -- Third Haven Meeting, Episcopal
source_profiles.repository: Swarthmore College Friends Historical Library / Maryland State Archives / Local parishes
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Third Haven Quaker Meeting (1682) is one of the oldest Quaker meetings in America — records at Swarthmore College; Episcopal parish records from 1692; Methodist records; Quaker manumission records document freed enslaved persons; Frederick Douglass references Talbot County churches

source_profiles.source_name: Talbot County Enslaved Persons Records -- MSA
source_profiles.repository: Maryland State Archives, Annapolis
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Frederick Douglass was born enslaved in Talbot County (c. 1818); estate inventories, slave auction records, manumission records, and free negro certificates at MSA; Wye House (Lloyd family) records document the plantation Douglass described in his autobiography

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Washington County formed 1776 from Frederick County during the Revolution — the first county in the US named for George Washington. County seat is Hagerstown. Located in the Cumberland Valley with strong German settlement. Key Civil War location (Battle of Antietam, 1862). The National Road passed through Hagerstown.
county_metadata.research_tips: MSA and Washington County Circuit Court (Hagerstown). Formed 1776 from Frederick County. Land records on mdlandrec.net from 1776. Hagerstown is a gateway to the Cumberland Valley with strong German settlement — German Reformed, Lutheran, and Church of the Brethren records are essential. The Washington County Historical Society holds extensive local records and the Western Maryland Room at the Washington County Free Library has genealogical resources. Antietam battlefield (1862) records at NARA document soldiers from the area. The National Road (Route 40) brought settlers westward. Conococheague settlement records predate county formation. Jonathan Hager (founder of Hagerstown) family papers are at the historical society.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Washington land records on mdlandrec.net; MSA has digitized deed books, mortgages, and land patents; one of the most complete online land record systems in the US

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Battle of Antietam (September 17, 1862) was the bloodiest single-day battle in American history — fought in Washington County; military records for local soldiers, hospital records, and burial records at NARA and Antietam National Cemetery

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Wicomico County formed 1867 from Somerset and Worcester counties. County seat is Salisbury, the commercial center of the Lower Eastern Shore. Named for the Wicomico River. Post-Civil War county whose pre-1867 records are split between Somerset and Worcester counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: MSA and Wicomico County Circuit Court (Salisbury). Formed 1867 from Somerset and Worcester counties — check both parent counties for pre-1867 records. Land records on mdlandrec.net from 1867. Wicomico County is the commercial center of the Lower Eastern Shore. The Edward H. Nabb Research Center for Delmarva History and Culture at Salisbury University is an excellent regional resource for Eastern Shore genealogy. Salisbury had significant timber and later poultry industries. The Wicomico County Historical Society maintains local records. African American church records (AME and Baptist) document the post-Civil War community. Check Somerset County records for pre-1867 Wicomico area families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Wicomico land records on mdlandrec.net; MSA has digitized deed books, mortgages, and land patents; one of the most complete online land record systems in the US

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Worcester County formed 1742 from Somerset County. County seat is Snow Hill. Maryland's only Atlantic Ocean county, including Ocean City. Named for the Earl of Worcester. Economy based on agriculture, fishing, timber, and later tourism.
county_metadata.research_tips: MSA and Worcester County Circuit Court (Snow Hill). Formed 1742 from Somerset County — check Somerset for pre-1742 records. Land records on mdlandrec.net from 1742. Worcester County is Maryland's only Atlantic Ocean county, including Ocean City. The Worcester County Library in Snow Hill has local history resources. Julia A. Purnell Museum in Snow Hill holds local artifacts and some records. Strong Episcopalian heritage — All Hallows Church, Snow Hill (1692) has colonial parish records. Significant enslaved population and free Black community on the Lower Eastern Shore. The county's coastal economy included fishing, timber, and later tourism. Pocomoke City area families often appear in both Worcester and Somerset records. Check the Nabb Research Center at Salisbury University for Delmarva regional resources.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Worcester land records on mdlandrec.net; MSA has digitized deed books, mortgages, and land patents; one of the most complete online land record systems in the US
